content.intro: Your project moves along a controlled process, from the first conversation to shipping.

items.texte: We listen to your project, question your choices of materials and cuts, and frame a realistic development together.
Your designs take shape on paper: precise patterns, grading, cutting templates ready for the workshop.
A first piece is made, tried on and adjusted — as many rounds as needed before you approve it.
The run starts in our Moroccan workshop: cutting, assembly and finishing, true to the approved prototype.
You are kept informed of progress; we watch deadlines and conformity throughout the run.
Every piece is checked before shipping. You receive a finished product, ready to carry your label.

items.question: I am launching my first brand: can you support me?
items.reponse: That is exactly what the workshop exists for. You do not need to arrive with a complete technical file: we start from your idea, even at sketch stage, and move it forward step by step — consulting, pattern making, prototype, then production. You approve each milestone before the next one starts.

items.question: Where are the garments made?
items.reponse: Development and communication are handled from Nîmes, in the south of France; production takes place in our workshop in Morocco. This setup lets us offer reasonable prices while keeping systematic quality control before every shipment.

items.question: How does the quote work?
items.reponse: Describe your project through the contact form, by phone or on WhatsApp: type of pieces, quantities you have in mind, target deadlines. The quote is free and without commitment — and it doubles as a first consulting conversation about the feasibility of your collection.

items.question: Do you work with clients abroad?
items.reponse: Yes. We reply in English and in French, and the whole process — pattern approvals, prototype photos, production follow-up — can be handled remotely.

content.titre: Let's bring your designs to life.
content.sousTitre: Tell us about your collection, your volumes and your deadlines: you get a free quote, no strings attached.

content.intro: Describe your pieces, quantities and deadlines: we come back to you quickly with a free quote.
content.formIntro: Free and without commitment. The more precise your description, the more accurate the quote.
services: Full project, from idea to finished product

content.titre: Services à la carte, or from idea to finished product
content.intro: Some clients hand us one precise step — a pattern, a run to produce. Others arrive with a sketch and leave with their collection. Both paths exist.

items.description: The step that prevents false starts: choice of materials, feasibility of the cuts, framing of the development budget. You leave with a clear plan before committing to production.

image.alt: Thread spools and sewing scissors on a worktop

items.description: From sketch to graded pattern, then to a prototype fitted and reworked. Nothing goes into production until the sample piece satisfies you.

image.alt: Drawing a pattern with a ruler, with fashion sketches and fabric swatches on the workbench

image.alt: Seamstress assembling a garment on a sewing machine

items.description: Regular progress updates during the run, then a piece-by-piece check before shipping: you always know where your production stands.

image.alt: Checking brand labels and a garment's technical spec sheet

content.titre: One thread, from first conversation to shipping
paragraphes: When a garment development goes off the rails, it is almost always because a step was skipped: a run launched without an approved prototype, a rough pattern patched up at the sewing machine, quality checked on arrival instead of at the source. Our method is to never skip a step — but to size each one to your project. A brand taking its first steps does not have the same needs as a professional outsourcing a well-oiled production.

Concretely, every milestone ends with your sign-off: the development plan after consulting, the pattern after pattern making, the sample piece after prototyping. Only once the prototype is approved does production start in our Moroccan workshop, and the product you receive matches what you approved — guaranteeing that is exactly what quality control is for.

You keep a single point of contact from start to finish, reachable by phone, WhatsApp or email, in English as in French. Working remotely is our everyday routine: photo-based approvals, detailed feedback, regular progress updates throughout production.

content.titre: One precise step, or the whole journey?
content.sousTitre: Tell us where your project stands: the quote is free and clarifies things either way.

meta.title: Collection consulting and development
meta.description: Collection framing before production: choice of materials, feasibility of the cuts, development plan. The consulting step that spares young brands false starts.

content.intro: Before cutting any fabric, you need to know exactly what to produce, in which material, and at what cost. That is what this step is for.

content.titre: Why frame a project when you already have the ideas
paragraphes: Most designers who contact us know very well what they want: they have the sketches, the references, sometimes a full moodboard. What is rarely missing is the vision; what is often missing is its industrial translation. Which fabric drapes like the reference picture, yet stays within your budget? Does this cut require a specific machine? Will this finishing detail hold up in production, or only on a one-off piece sewn by hand?

Consulting means running your project through these questions, one by one, before they turn into costly problems. We examine each design, challenge the choices that will cause trouble, and suggest alternatives whenever a material or a construction is likely to disappoint. We sometimes advise against a piece — that too is what a supporting workshop is for.

You leave with a development plan: the shortlisted designs, the intended materials, the steps ahead and their order. That document then guides the pattern making and the production — and if you decide to take the next steps to another workshop, it remains yours.
This step is aimed first at young brands preparing their first collection, but professionals use it too, to arbitrate a new range or industrialise an existing design.

image.alt: Thread spools, scissors and measuring tape on the workshop worktop

items.question: Do I need finished sketches before consulting you?
items.reponse: No. Polished sketches speed things up, but we can start from reference pictures, from existing garments you want to adapt, or from an idea described in plain words. Framing is precisely about turning that starting point into a concrete plan.

items.question: Can you advise me on fabric choices?
items.reponse: Yes, it is at the heart of this step: we point you towards materials consistent with the drape you are after, the garment's use and your budget, and we flag the ones that would complicate production or weaken the finished product.

items.question: Does consulting commit me to producing with you?
items.reponse: No. The development plan belongs to you. Most of our clients naturally move on to pattern making and production at the workshop, but that is a logical next step, not a condition.

items.description: The approved development plan turns into patterns, then into a sample piece.
Once the prototype is approved, the workshop takes over for the run.

content.titre: Put your project on the table.
content.sousTitre: A first conversation is often enough to know whether your collection is ready for production.

service.name: Pattern making & prototyping
service.serviceType: Garment pattern making, grading and prototyping

content.intro: The pattern is your garment's score: if it is right, everything that follows is too. If it is approximate, the whole run inherits it.
A clear rate based on the complexity of the design. Every pattern is made to measure, ready for production.
content.note: Indicative rates — a precise quote is drawn up according to the complexity of each design.

items.nom: Pattern adjustment and correction after fitting

items.question: I already have my patterns: can you produce directly?
items.reponse: Yes, if your patterns are usable as they are. We examine them before quoting: if they need cleaning up or grading, we tell you and include it in the quote rather than discovering the problem mid-run.

items.question: How many alterations are possible on the prototype?
items.reponse: As many as the piece requires. The goal is not to cap the back-and-forth but to reach a prototype you approve without reservation, because it will serve as the reference for the whole production.

items.question: Can I follow the fittings remotely?
items.reponse: Yes. Detailed photos, videos of the drape, measurements taken on the piece: remote approval is part of how we work, in English as in French.

items.description: If the project deserves framing first: materials, feasibility, development plan.
The approved prototype goes into production in our Moroccan workshop.

content.titre: Your design deserves an accurate pattern.
content.sousTitre: Send us your sketches or your existing patterns: we will tell you what it takes to bring them to production.

meta.title: Garment production in Morocco
meta.description: Cutting, assembly and finishing in our Moroccan workshop, faithful to the approved prototype. Tracked production and reasonable prices for brands and professionals.

content.intro: This is where your collection stops being a project: cutting, assembly, finishing, in our garment workshop in Morocco.

content.titre: A workshop in Morocco, steered from Nîmes
paragraphes: Our organisation rests on two complementary anchors. In Nîmes, your single point of contact: she has known your project since the first conversation, approves every milestone with you and reports on progress. In Morocco, the garment workshop: that is where your pieces are cut, assembled and finished, by hands whose trade is sewing.

This setup is not a compromise, it is a deliberate choice: it allows reasonable prices without cutting corners on execution. The counterpart is on us — close production follow-up and systematic quality control, because a remote production cannot be steered blindfolded.

The run starts solely on the basis of the prototype you approved: same materials, same measurements, same finishings. During production you receive regular progress updates; at the end of the run, every piece is checked before shipping. What you unbox matches what you signed off.

Young brands and professionals alike entrust us with their production: first collections to launch, ranges to renew or manufacturing to outsource. Every project is priced on a quote, free of charge, based on the pieces, quantities and deadlines.

image.alt: Seamstress assembling a garment on a machine in the workshop

items.question: How do you keep control over a remote production?
items.reponse: Through the approved prototype, which serves as the contractual reference for the run, through regular progress updates during production, and through piece-by-piece quality control before shipping. Follow-up is not an option with us: it is part of the production.

items.question: Who supplies the fabrics and trims?
items.reponse: Both cases exist: you can supply your materials, or entrust us with sourcing them when the project is framed. This point is settled at the quote, so the run starts with no grey area.

items.question: What kinds of pieces do you manufacture?
items.reponse: The workshop works on garments in the broad sense. The simplest is to describe your designs: we quickly confirm feasibility, and we tell you just as frankly if a piece falls outside our craft.

items.description: The mandatory step before any run: the reference piece the production will follow.
The detail of what we check during and after production.

content.titre: Your run, sewn like the sample piece.
content.sousTitre: Describe your designs, quantities and deadlines: the quote is free.

meta.title: Garment quality control and follow-up
meta.description: Progress updates during the run, piece-by-piece checks before shipping, conformity to the approved prototype: your production's safety net.

service.name: Quality control & follow-up
service.serviceType: Garment quality control and production follow-up

content.intro: A good run is not observed on delivery: it is built during production. That is what follow-up is for — and the final check.

content.titre: Check at the source rather than discover on arrival
paragraphes: The worst moment to discover a defect is while unboxing. A puckered seam, a size off the measurement chart, a finish different from the sample piece: at that stage, every problem costs time, money and sometimes a launch. Our answer holds in two words — track and check — and it is included in every production of the workshop, not sold as an extra.

Follow-up starts as soon as the run is launched. You receive regular progress updates, grounded in what actually comes off the machines, while we monitor conformity to the approved prototype: materials, measurements, assembly, finishings. A deviation caught during production gets corrected on the ongoing run; the same deviation discovered at delivery turns into a negotiation — that is the whole difference.

At the end of production, every piece goes through a final check before packing: overall appearance, seams, measurements at the garment's key points, finishing ready to carry your label. Only after that does the run ship. The finished product you receive is the one you approved — that is the commitment closing our process, and the reason clients entrust us with remote productions.

image.alt: Checking brand labels and a garment's technical spec sheet

items.question: Is quality control billed on top of production?
items.reponse: No. Production follow-up and the final check are an integral part of every run made at the workshop: we would not conceive of shipping pieces that have not been verified.

items.question: What happens if a piece fails the check?
items.reponse: It is reworked before shipping. That is precisely the point of checking at the workshop rather than on reception: the defect gets fixed where it was born, without parcel round-trips or disputes.

items.question: What exactly does the final check cover?
items.reponse: Conformity to the approved prototype: measurements at the garment's key points, regularity of seams and finishings, the overall appearance of the piece and its final presentation, ready to reach your shelves.

items.description: The run that follow-up frames, cut and assembled in our Moroccan workshop.
The approved prototype: the reference every piece is compared to.

content.sousTitre: Entrust us with your run: it will be tracked during production and checked piece by piece before it ships.

This is a moment rarely filmed — here it is, with no needless editing: the gesture, the fabric, the pace.

A single point of contact in France, production under control: you follow progress without piling up middlemen.
